name: Willie Smith
abstract: William McLeish Smith (November 25, 1910 – March 7, 1967) was an American saxophonist and one of the major alto saxophone players of the swing era. He also played clarinet and sang.

works.t: No No Nanette: Tea for Two

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: The three alto saxophonists who were principal players in these bands became my idols at the time: from the Lunceford orchestra, Willie Smith
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Norris Turney
about_quotes.evidence: I was always being influenced by Johnny Hodges and Benny Carter and Willie Smith
about_quotes.doc_title: Norris Turney interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Young Snooky
about_quotes.evidence: Willie and I was really good friends. Willie, I loved Willie, he loved me too.
about_quotes.doc_title: Young Snooky — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: James Chirillo
about_quotes.evidence: who was Cannonball listening to? Willie Smith, Benny Carter, Johnny Hodges
about_quotes.doc_title: James Chirillo interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: Well in that band they had Willie Smith playing lead alto... So all three of us went to Harry James and we said... we have a chance to join Duke Ellington’s band... Carney, Willie Smith, Johnny Hodges...
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 12, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Jack Lesberg
about_quotes.evidence: Maxie Kaminsky had just come back from with Artie Shaw’s band and he put a band together and it was Willie Smith and Rod Kless I remember, myself, and a couple of guys
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack Lesberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997
